title: FortiGate SD-WAN Tunnel Health
spl:
index=network sourcetype="fortinet:fortigate_event" (subtype="vpn" OR logdesc="*SD-WAN*")
| stats latest(state) as state by interface, neighbor
| search state IN ("down","dead","inactive")
description: SD-WAN tunnel health ensures branch connectivity and path failover behave as designed.
value: SD-WAN tunnel health ensures branch connectivity and path failover behave as designed.
implementation: Ingest event logs for SD-WAN and IPsec state changes. Build expected neighbor inventory. Page on tunnel down beyond SLA.
visualization: Status table (tunnels), Single value (down count), Timeline (state changes).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Sessions.All_Sessions by All_Sessions.action, All_Sessions.src, All_Sessions.user | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: IPS and firewall hits from authorized vulnerability scanners, Fortinet sandbox tests, and scheduled config or policy validation.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_fortinet`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=fortinet:fortigate_event`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest event logs for SD-WAN and IPsec state changes. Build expected neighbor inventory. Page on tunnel down beyond SLA.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype="fortinet:fortigate_event" (subtype="vpn" OR logdesc="*SD-WAN*")
| stats latest(state) as state by interface, neighbor
| search state IN ("down","dead","inactive")
```

Understanding this SPL

**FortiGate SD-WAN Tunnel Health** — SD-WAN tunnel health ensures branch connectivity and path failover behave as designed.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=fortinet:fortigate_event`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_fortinet`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: fortinet:fortigate_event. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: Anomaly — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype="fortinet:fortigate_event". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by interface, neighbor** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Sessions.All_Sessions by All_Sessions.action, All_Sessions.src, All_Sessions.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**FortiGate SD-WAN Tunnel Health** — SD-WAN tunnel health ensures branch connectivity and path failover behave as designed.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=fortinet:fortigate_event`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_fortinet`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Detection type** for this use case: Anomaly — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Sessions.All_Sessions` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over a window that includes both normal production traffic and, if you have one, a documented test (vendor sandbox, lab rule hit, or exercise). Compare a few sample events to the vendor’s own console or reports and tune indexes, fields, and thresholds so alerts are actionable before paging.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Status table (tunnels), Single value (down count), Timeline (state changes).
grandmaExplanation: We use FortiGate data and alerts to stay ahead of the risks described in "FortiGate SD-WAN Tunnel Health". We help the team notice trouble early, while a situation is still small and containable.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Network_Sessions.All_Sessions` dataset after normalizing this feed to the model; otherwise the `cimSpl` tstats search may return no rows.
